Title: Cytokinin levels and signaling respond to wounding and the perception of herbivore elicitors in Nicotiana attenuata

Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: iraoc line A-07-457; WT Utah inbred line
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: JA-biosynthesis-impaired transgenic line used for treatment comparisons
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Cross-talk between jasmonate and cytokinin pathways during herbivory responses
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Silencing AOC altered herbivory-induced CK metabolite levels and enhanced NaRRA5 transcript accumulation, showing JA suppresses CK responses
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNAi line comparison with WT; CK metabolite profiling; NaRRA5 transcript analysis
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating JA biosynthesis alters CK-defense cross-talk, but not a direct CK metabolism gene
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: AOC is not a CK-pathway gene; included because it was directly manipulated to interpret CK pathway regulation
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AOC-mediated JA biosynthesis is not required to induce CK responses. 2. JA deficiency modifies specific CK metabolite levels. 3. JA pathway activity suppresses CK signaling readout NaRRA5 under herbivory simulation.

Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: ircoi1 line A-04-249; WT Utah inbred line
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: JA-signaling-impaired transgenic line used for treatment comparisons
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Integrates JA-CK cross-talk during herbivory responses
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Impairment of COI1 altered herbivory-induced CK metabolite levels and enhanced NaRRA5 accumulation, supporting JA-mediated suppression of CK signaling
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNAi line comparison with WT; CK metabolite profiling; NaRRA5 transcript analysis
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential leverage point for hormone cross-talk engineering, though not direct nutrient metabolism target
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: COI1 is not part of CK metabolism/signaling itself but was central for testing JA suppression of CK responses
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. COI1-dependent JA perception is not required for CK induction by herbivory simulation. 2. Loss of COI1 enhances CK signaling marker induction. 3. JA signaling acts antagonistically on parts of the CK response.

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Leaf tissues after wounding and simulated herbivory; transcriptionally regulated over hours after treatment
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Local and systemic leaves in response to wounding and Manduca sexta oral secretions
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Initial CK biosynthetic step producing isopentenylated CK nucleotides
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers an isopentenyl group to adenine nucleotides to initiate cytokinin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Cytokinins including IPR and cZR-responsive CK pool
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves; also systemic leaves and roots show CK changes after local treatment
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Wound- and herbivore-associated molecular pattern-responsive signaling; likely contributes to local and systemic herbivory responses
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: NaIPT5 transcripts are regulated by wounding, indicating contribution of CK biosynthetic capacity to herbivory-induced cytokinin accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Leaf wounding; Manduca sexta oral secretions; exogenous tZ/cZR spraying
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Herbivory-regulated microarray; qPCR confirmation; correlation with CK metabolite profiling after treatments
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Microarray (Agilent Nicotiana attenuata platform; GEO GPL13527); Transcriptome Shotgun Assembly accession GBGF01000000
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to manipulate cytokinin biosynthesis during herbivory responses, though direct engineering value for nutrient traits was not tested
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: NaIPT5 was downregulated by exogenous tZ and cZR, supporting CK-mediated feedback regulation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NaIPT5 is a cytokinin biosynthetic gene responsive to wounding. 2. Its transcript changes indicate rapid remodeling of CK precursor synthesis after herbivore elicitation. 3. Exogenous CK application suppresses NaIPT5 transcripts, consistent with feedback regulation.

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Rapidly regulated in wounded local leaves within first hours
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Local leaves after wounding and oral secretion elicitation
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Activation of CK nucleotides to free-base CKs
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts cytokinin nucleotides directly into active free-base cytokinins by phosphoribohydrolase activity
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Rapid NaLOG1 transcriptional regulation is associated with early accumulation of active CKs after wounding and herbivore elicitation
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor/directly forms active product pool
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Microarray kinetics; qPCR confirmation of representative genes; CK metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Microarray GEO GPL13527; transcriptome assembly GBGF01000000
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate to modulate rapid CK activation in defense-related signaling
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Authors infer rapid increases in some LOG transcripts may elevate CK levels and counteract inactivation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NaLOG1 is rapidly regulated after wounding and simulated herbivory. 2. It likely contributes to early active CK accumulation. 3. Its response supports activation of CK biosynthesis during herbivore perception.

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Rapidly regulated in local leaves; also altered in systemic tissues
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Local leaves, systemic leaves, and roots after wounding and oral secretion application
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Activation of CK nucleotides to free-base CKs
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Releases active free-base cytokinins from cytokinin monophosphate precursors
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Local and systemic leaves; roots show pathway changes
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports local and long-distance herbivory-responsive CK signaling
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: NaLOG4 responds to local and systemic herbivory-related treatments, linking CK activation with both local and systemic responses
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Microarray kinetics in local and systemic tissues; qPCR confirmation of representative genes; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Microarray GEO GPL13527; transcriptome assembly GBGF01000000
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for tuning systemic CK responses to herbivory
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Highlighted as representative biosynthesis gene for systemic transcript changes
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NaLOG4 is differentially regulated by wounding and OS treatment. 2. Its expression also changes in systemic tissues. 3. It supports the conclusion that CK activation contributes to systemic herbivory signaling.

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Long-term regulation lasting frequently more than 20 h after treatment
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Local leaves after wounding and OS application
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Activation of CK nucleotides to free-base CKs
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts inactive cytokinin nucleotide precursors to active free bases
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Contributes to sustained CK response after herbivory-related stimuli
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: NaLOG5 shows prolonged transcript regulation after treatment, suggesting involvement in maintaining CK changes
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Microarray GEO GPL13527; transcriptome assembly GBGF01000000
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mentioned as long-term responsive transcript rather than directly functionally manipulated
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NaLOG5 displays sustained transcriptional responses after wounding/herbivory simulation. 2. This pattern suggests a role in maintaining prolonged CK pathway activation. 3. Direct causal manipulation was not performed.

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Rapidly induced in local leaves; also changes systemically
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Leaves, systemic leaves, and roots after wounding and oral secretion application
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Irreversibly degrades cytokinins by oxidatively cleaving the side chain
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Shapes amplitude and duration of CK signaling after wounding and herbivore elicitation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: NaCKX5 is strongly induced by wounding and OS, indicating activation of CK degradation during herbivory responses
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Wounding; Manduca sexta oral secretions; exogenous tZ/cZR spraying
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Microarray kinetics; qPCR confirmation; CK treatment response; metabolite profiling correlation
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Microarray GEO GPL13527; transcriptome assembly GBGF01000000
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to prolong or dampen herbivory-induced CK signals, but agronomic utility not tested
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: NaCKX5 transcripts were upregulated by exogenous CK application, consistent with feedback control
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NaCKX5 is rapidly induced by wounding and simulated herbivory. 2. It likely mediates feedback degradation of elevated CKs. 3. Systemic expression changes indicate CK turnover is remodeled across the plant.

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Rapidly induced in local leaves after treatment
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Leaves after wounding and OS application
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Cytokinin O-glucosides such as tZROG/cZROG-related inactive forms
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers glucose to zeatin-type cytokinins to form O-glucosides and reduce active CK pool
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Represents cytokinin inactivation/remodeling during wound and herbivore responses
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: NaZOG1 induction is associated with increased CK glucoside accumulation after wounding and herbivory simulation
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for storage/modification of target product
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Microarray GEO GPL13527; transcriptome assembly GBGF01000000
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Authors suggest changes in ZOG expression may account for rises in inactivation products
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NaZOG1 is rapidly induced by wounding and OS treatment. 2. Its induction coincides with increased CK glucosides. 3. It likely contributes to CK inactivation during herbivore responses.

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Rapidly induced in local leaves after treatment
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Leaves after wounding and OS application
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Glucosylates zeatin-type cytokinins to produce inactive/storage forms
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Modulates cytokinin homeostasis during herbivory responses
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: NaZOG2 induction suggests enhanced CK inactivation following wounding and HAMP perception
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for storage/modification of target product
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Microarray GEO GPL13527; transcriptome assembly GBGF01000000
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Highlighted among particularly induced inactivation enzymes
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NaZOG2 is induced by wounding and OS elicitation. 2. It likely contributes to the increased glucosylated CK forms observed. 3. The study supports pathway-wide CK inactivation remodeling during herbivory.

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Rapidly induced in local leaves after treatment
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Leaves after wounding and OS application
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Conjugates glucose to zeatin-type cytokinins for inactivation/storage
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: CK homeostasis adjustment during wounding and herbivore elicitation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: NaZOG3 is induced by wounding and OS treatment, consistent with enhanced cytokinin glucosylation
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for storage/modification of target product
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Microarray GEO GPL13527; transcriptome assembly GBGF01000000
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Interpreted together with CK glucoside accumulation rather than individually functionally dissected
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NaZOG3 is rapidly induced by herbivory-related treatments. 2. Its expression is consistent with elevated CK glucoside levels. 3. It forms part of the induced CK inactivation program.

Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Rapidly induced in local leaves by wounding and strongly amplified by oral secretions; also changed systemically
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Local leaves, systemic leaves, and roots after wounding and simulated herbivory
Regulation_Genes.Regulator_Type: Signal transduction regulator / cytokinin response regulator
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Cytokinin signaling output and feedback inhibition
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: CK-responsive genes in cytokinin signaling pathway; exact direct targets not identified
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Acts as a marker of activated CK signaling; type-A RRs mediate feedback inhibition of CK signaling
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Feedback inhibition within CK signaling pathway
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Wounding; Manduca sexta oral secretions; synthetic FACs; exogenous tZ and cZR; jasmonate pathway status; MeJA pretreatment
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Integral component of wound- and HAMP-triggered signaling and systemic herbivory responses
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Used as the principal CK-responsive marker gene demonstrating activation of CK signaling by wounding, FACs, and herbivore elicitors
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: NaRRA5 transcripts strongly increase after wounding and especially OS/FAC treatment, indicating amplified CK signaling; JA suppresses this response
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Leaf wounding; Manduca sexta oral secretions; FAC application; MeJA pretreatment; JA-pathway-deficient iraoc and ircoi1 lines
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Microarray kinetics; qPCR; FAC elicitation assay; CK spray application; comparison in JA-pathway transgenic lines and MeJA treatment
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: Microarray GEO GPL13527; transcriptome assembly GBGF01000000
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful marker and possible target for engineering CK-mediated herbivory responses, but direct trait engineering was not tested
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Authors explicitly used NaRRA5 as CK-responsive marker gene based on strong responsiveness to CK and herbivory elicitors
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NaRRA5 is a robust marker of CK signaling activated by wounding and strongly amplified by herbivore elicitors/FACs. 2. Exogenous CKs also induce NaRRA5, validating it as a CK-response readout. 3. JA pathway impairment enhances, whereas MeJA suppresses, NaRRA5 induction, revealing antagonistic JA-CK interaction.

Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Differentially regulated with long-term effects frequently lasting more than 20 h
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Local leaves after wounding and oral secretion treatment
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: CK signal transfer from receptors to response regulators
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Type-A and type-B response regulators in CK signaling cascade
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Relays CK perception signal to downstream response regulators
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Part of multistep histidine kinase phosphorelay
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Transduces wound- and herbivory-responsive CK signaling
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Contributes to pathway-wide signaling activation, but not identified as master regulator
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: NaHPT2 transcript changes indicate that CK phosphorelay signaling components are transcriptionally remodeled by herbivory-related cues
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: Microarray GEO GPL13527; transcriptome assembly GBGF01000000
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as representative signaling component; direct functional manipulation was not performed
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NaHPT2 is differentially regulated after wounding and herbivore elicitation. 2. Its prolonged response indicates sustained remodeling of CK phosphorelay signaling. 3. This supports the conclusion that the signaling arm of the CK pathway responds to herbivory.
